> They still haven't fixed their unit planner. FWIW the IB pacing guide looks a lot saner to me than the AP curriculum guide.
replies.original_text: > They still have not fixed their build system. FWIW the Firefox build instructions [1] look a lot saner to me than the Chromium build instructions [2]. [1] https://firefox-source-docs.mozilla.org/setup/linux_build.ht... [2] https://chromium.googlesource.com/chromium/src/+/main/docs/l...

Some mozilla software developer I guess. They still have not fixed their build system. Meson/ninja or cmake would be alternatives. Nothing to have them abandon mozconfig ... this is legacy code. The rest of the world moved on. Mozilla lives in the past.

replies.text: That would be John, a legend in the ESL department. Worked with him on the writing curriculum in 2007 and the DP program in 2009; veterans of the language team will never forget his hack day entry: a lesson on 'Mac and Me' using a toaster oven.
What is he notable for in the context of IB?
Maybe read the blog post? Crazy suggestion for a teacher, I know.
replies.original_text: Maybe read the blog post? Crazy suggestion, I know.
What is he notable for in the context of Mozilla?

That would be JR Conlin, national treasure. Worked with him on YDN in 2007 and Netflix in 2009; veterans of the Netflix API team will never forget his hack day entry, which was "Mac and Me" playing on a toaster oven.

comments.original_text: Who is this person?

comments.text: We're a niche IB school that is lucky enough to get well funded. Now we really need a viable alternative to the Evil College Board Empire. For a while I had hope that the 'Open Curriculum' project would be that competitor, but that died after I was banned from the teacher forum, as well as the principal making some really strange decisions in the last year or so. I gave up on the IB many years ago, though. The key insight was when one IB coordinator explained that all teachers use ManageBac + Google Classroom. So using Canvas (which annoys me because the College Board controls it too), I had no audio...

replies.text: Curriculum options have a maintenance cost. ManageBac is the current IB LMS, like Toddle was before when it replaced paper planners.
replies.original_text: Options have a maintenance cost. Pulseaudio is the current Linux audio stack, like plain ALSA was before when it replaced OSS.

replies.text: Hey, we have the evil Apple empire :) Or the Google alternative. Maintaining a curriculum including updating for the latest changes in IB standards when someone points a new framework at your worksheets is a really hard problem. Someone should convince the government to look into funding a new alternative to the IB, maybe.
replies.original_text: Hey, we have the evil Microsoft empire :) Or the Apple alternative. Maintaining a browser engine including patching the latest vulnerabilities when someone points Mythos at your code is a really hard problem, my feeling is you need a certain size of organization and funding as your table stakes. Someone should convince the EU to look into funding a new browser, maybe.

comments.original_text: > We're a niche browser that is lucky enough to get well funded. Now - we really need a viable alternative to the Evil Google Empire. For a while I had hope that ladybird would be that competitor, but that died after I was banned from github, as well as Kling making some really strange decisions in the last year or so, with weird explanations; most recent one the "we don't need external contributors so we close that down" (in part also due to the rise of AI slop spam, which is indeed annoying, but Kling is a strange guy really). I gave up on Mozilla many years ago already, though. The key insight I had was when one mozilla dev explaind that all linux guys use systemd + pulseaudio. So, using youtube (which annoys me because the evil Google empire controls it as well), I had no audio on...

comments.text: Interesting to read, but ultimately it's very easy to blame 'administrators' for everything. If the IB didn't try out these new initiatives, if the IB spent all money on teacher training only, if the IB made the best curriculum ever, would that really make a difference? Would more schools use it? IB is surviving on the mercy of school fees, it's not a viable strategy. Enrollment has been declining for a decade. Doing nothing might lead to an even more rapid decline.

replies.text: The leaders could, for example, have made the new compulsory online assessment opt-in. If it's popular, make it the default for new schools later. Instead we had to go a few versions from 'now with online exams' to 'now with an offline option' because they got enough negative teacher feedback. I don't mind experiments, but if you're the 'puts students in control' curriculum then please build an off switch in from the start.
Online assessments are not the reason why the average student moved away from IB. Online exams are however a potential avenue for raising money.
Oh, I agree - IB was losing enrolment long before online exams were a thing. I meant to use that as a recent example of the kind of decisions that IB leadership repeatedly makes.
replies.original_text: Oh, I agree - firefox was losing market share long before AI was a thing. I meant to use that as a recent example of the kind of decisions that Mozilla leadership repeatedly makes, that don't match up what their users want.
AI is not, and was not the reason why the average user moved away from Firefox. AI is however a potential avenue for raising money.

replies.text: Again: Would it have made a measurable difference? Or is it just moaning from a small core? Not saying the core is not important, but I don't think the IB can survive on only us.

> don't think IB can survive on only us. not at the current employee and costs. But do they need to do that? Do they need to produce new programs (and pay the cost to do so)? Why can't they be lean and mean? Focus purely on curriculum experience without any BS, without any upsell? And there are volunteer teachers out there that willingly contribute lessons for free.
replies.original_text: > don't think Fx can survive on only us. not at the current employee and costs. But do they need to do that? Do they need to produce new products (and pay the cost to do so)? Why can't they be lean and mean? Focus purely on browser experience without any BS, without any upsell? And there are volunteers out there that willingly contribute code/fixes for free.
Again: Would it have made a measurable difference? Or is it just moaning from a small core? Not saying the core is not important, but I don't think Fx can survive on only us.

The leaders could, for example, have made AI opt-in. If it's popular, maybe make it the default for new installs later on. Instead we had to go a few versions from "now with AI" to "now with an AI off button" because they got enough negative user feedback. I don't mind experiments, but if you're the "we put you back in control" browser then please build an "off" switch in from the start.

replies.text: A curriculum like the IB depends on word of mouth among teachers. There was not a single announcement or decision by the IB leadership in the last 10 years that would make me recommend the IB to other schools, instead every time it pushed me away a little bit more. I have hardly ever seen such fundamental alienation of their core audience, even for education standards.

The swift deprecation of the old assessment criteria felt like a kick in the... and made me switch to the French Bac for a while, after which I landed on the IB's older syllabus to avoid the immediate impact of bad decisions. Part of the struggle was my fight against the web as a platform, with its many privacy and security issues that accumulated as W3C APIs were added. Nowadays, a lot of the privacy controls have landed in the IB proper, in part thanks to the teacher community upstreaming, if you know where to look.
replies.original_text: This, in part. The swift deprecation of XUL extensions felt like a kick in the gonads and made me switch to Pale Moon for a while, after which I landed on Firefox ESR to avoid the inmediate impact of bad decisions, and accumulated a veritable landslide of user.js and userchrome.css tweaks I keep having to maintain. On the other hand, part of the struggle was my fight against the web as a """platform""", with its many privacy and security issues that accumulated as W3C APIs were added like hot cakes and websites exploded in complexity. Firefox provided the control necessary through addons, thanks to its vast community of likeminded people. Nowadays, a lot of the privacy controls have landed in firefox proper, in part thanks to the tor browser upstreaming, if you know where to look.

> But wouldn't it perhaps just have lead to an even more rapid decline? A product like Firefox depends on word of mouth. There was not a *single* announcement or decision by the Mozilla leadership in the last 10 years or so which would make me recommend Firefox to others, instead every single time it pushed me away a little bit more. I have hardly ever seen such a fundamental alienation of their core audience, even for Silicon Valley standards ;)

replies.text: The problem is what 'veteran teachers' want is typically opposed to what is needed at the time to improve the curriculum, such as: * Wanting niche features that benefit other people than those in the veteran core, thus preventing the IB from gaining market share. * Ever-increasing expectations in terms of visible feature delivery (e.g., the shift to TOK was widely seen as a failure despite being foundational to move to a more critical thinking model). * General conservativeness in terms of anything that breaks workflows. * Most importantly, lack of proposals for monetization from said audience (donations do not cut it and smaller and more important projects like OpenSSL...).

I didn't hear any teachers complain about the new online learning platform integration, which helps fund the IB. A paid professional development service is something people have been asking for a while.
replies.original_text: I didn't hear any enthusiasts complain about the new VPN integration, which helps fund the browser. A paid email service is something people have been asking for a while. People event want to donate money for browser development, but can't due to the foundation structure.

The problem is what "enthusiasts" want is typically opposed to what is needed at the time to improve the product, such as: * Wanting niche features that benefit other people than those in the enthusiast core, thus preventing the company from gaining market share and revenue. * Ever-increasing expectations in terms of visible feature delivery (e.g. e10s was widely seen as a failure despite being foundational to move off a single thread model and increase browser responsiveness). * General conservativeness in terms of anything that breaks workflows (famously [1], but also see the criticism of Firefox redesigns over the years, etc.) * Most importantly, lack of proposals for monetization from said audience (donations do not cut it and smaller and more important projects such as OpenSSL,...

comments.original_text: Interesting to read, but ultimately it's very easy to blame "leaders" for everything and I'm not sure it has much merit. It's popular to pile on them and their decisions. But I don't think it's as obvious as people (often here on HN) make it out to be. If Mozilla didn't try out these avenues deemed wrong, if Mozilla spent all money on the browser only, if Mozilla made the best browser ever, would that really make a difference? Would more people use it, would they be a healthier organization now? Mozilla is surviving on the mercy of Google money, it's not a viable strategy. Firefox usage has been declining for a decade. Doing nothing, or just doing the exact same as before, is popular with its fans (including me). But wouldn't it perhaps just have lead to an even more rapid decline?

comments.text: Respect. This is what the IB could have been. In the real world, there was a brief time when the 'puts you back in control' curriculum needed you to change the following settings in the IBIS portal to disable the force-pushed online proctoring: enable_online_proctoring, disable_camera_feed, disable_screen_monitoring, disable_browser_lockdown, enable_offline_mode, disable_ai_proctoring_model_download, disable_auto_record. A bit of community feedback later, and we've got one big 'off' button, and me wondering which footgun the executives will shoot themselves with next.

replies.text: And those are some of the better named settings in ManageBac. Some are pretty opaque and poorly documented. You can tell there isn't an edict to make settings highly accessible.
replies.original_text: And those are some of the better named config options. Some are pretty opaque, as are their values (and often poorly documented). You can tell there isn't an edict to make config options highly accessible

comments.original_text: Respect. This is what Firefox could have been. In the real world, in the same line as the article suggests, there was a brief time when the "puts you back in control" browser needed you to change the following about:config settings to disable the force-pushed ai: browser.ml.enable, browser.ml.chat.enabled, browser.ml.chat.sidebar, browser.ml.chat.menu, browser.ml.chat.page, extensions.ml.enabled, browser.ml.linkPreview.enabled, browser.ml.pageAssist.enabled, browser.ml.smartAssist.enabled, browser.tabs.groups.smart.enabled, browser.tabs.groups.smart.userEnabled, pdfjs.enableAltTextModelDownload, pdfjs.enableGuessAltText A bit of community feedback later, and we've got one big "off" button, and me wondering which footgun the executives will shoot themselves with next.

comments.text: Some 10 years ago I was an IB volunteer teacher. I mainly worked on curriculum development for the MYP, to the point of becoming a 'subject area expert'. Some of the work I did landed in the citations of a couple of papers about international education. They flew me to a workshop in Vancouver for an event where employees and volunteers worked together in the same room and they even made me attend a sort-of corporate meeting where they sort-of fought about something. But on the last day they announced that they were moving day-to-day conversations from the official IB forum (open to all) to a closed WhatsApp group. I felt betrayed.

replies.text: Do the IB really still need volunteers in this day and age? They are established enough not to need to exploit teacher goodwill.
As the OP says, the point is not that they needed unpaid work. The point is that volunteers shaped what the IB, MYP, DP were.
replies.original_text: As the OP says, the point is not that they needed unpaid work, if that's what you mean. The point is that volunteers shaped what Firefox, MDN, Thunderbird, Mozilla itself were.
Do Mozilla really still need volunteers in this day and age? Tbh even in 2015. They are established enough to not need to exploit goodwill.

comments.original_text: Some 10 years ago I was a Mozilla volunteer. I mainly worked on MDN, to the point of becoming a so-called "topic driver" for the glossary. Some of the work I did landed in the citations of a couple of papers about web technology. They flew me a whole week to Vancouver for an event where employees and volunteers worked together in the same room and they even made me (and the other volunteers ) attend a sort-of-corporate meeting where they sort-of fought about something (can't even remember what it was). I'm telling you this to highlight that volunteers where a huge part of Mozilla. But on the last day they announced that they were moving the day-to-day conversations from IRC (an open protocol) to Yahoo Messenger (a closed protocol). I felt sort of betrayed in that moment: the company...

comments.text: I think the IB started getting nuts the day they ventured with the Career-related Programme. To this day, I am still kind of confused with that move.
comments.original_text: I think Mozilla started getting nuts the day they ventured with Firefox OS. To this day, I am still kind of confused with that move.

comments.text: First of all thanks for posting what's on your mind and everything you did at the IB. Sorry to hear you are burnt out, hope you get better with time. I've been a loyal IB teacher since forever - reading, writing, the lesson planning I do is always within the IB framework. It's the first curriculum I always recommend to new schools. I'm grateful the IB exists, and the world of international education would be much worse if it wasn't around. I don't like that the IB is taking money from schools - I'd prefer if it was all community driven, to the point of a teacher-owned cooperative, but I'm probably delusional. Yet, I'm hopeful for the future.
comments.original_text: First of all thanks for posting what's on your mind and everything you did at Mozilla. Sorry to hear you are burnt out, hope you get better with time. I've been a loyal Firefox user since forever - reading, writing, web dev I do is always in Firefox. It's a first app I always install. I'm grateful Firefox exists, and the world (at least mine) would be much worse if it wasn't around. I don't like Mozilla is taking money from Google - I'd prefer if it was all community driven, to the point of a community owned co-operative, but I'm probably delusional. Yet, I'm hopeful for the future.

comments.text: “can it build a lesson plan idea I've had for years, in a single shot?” Do people do no research or introspection when they’ve had an “idea for years”? There are countless examples of this exact lesson plan. I taught this in my Grade 4 class! There’s like 50 of them on Teachers Pay Teachers right now. The standard “this almost certainly exists wholesale in the training data” applies, but I’m also interested in how you carry an idea for years and don’t notice this, or whether the “idea” here was actually “using this thing that’s been remade thousands of times as an AI benchmark”. There’s nothing wrong with remaking an old classic lesson plan, especially in teaching. It’s the describing it as “an idea I’ve had for years” that rings weird.

replies.text: While I agree that it isn't revolutionary that it could implement this from a single prompt, what's surprising to see is how well done this one is compared to the other tries. The scaffolding and flow are smooth, the differentiation isn't jittery, the assessment makes sense, there's a clear progression in difficulty. This model clearly "understands" the implementation of this lesson plan far better than the others did.
replies.original_text: While I agree that it isn't revolutionary that it could implement this from a single prompt, what's surprising to see is how well done this one is compared to the other tries. The controls and movement are smooth, the animations aren't jittery, the ui makes sense, there's a clear progression in difficulty. This model clearly "understands" the implementation of this game far better than the others did.

The question is more whether this lesson plan exists as open source somewhere in the training data (probably does).

replies.text: I think that's exactly why AI is suited for 99% of stuff we do. I have pointed out on here before that instances of truly unique human ideas not grounded in nature or previous ideas from others is almost nil, there are not many examples that someone can give me. All human ideas and work is derivative. Elves? Humans with pointy ears. Werewolves? Humans mixed with wolves. Car tyre? Cart wheel...stone wheel/roller. Etc. All lesson plans are derivative. Interactive notebooks? Worksheets with flaps. PBL? Real-world projects. Etc.

I feel like prior to GenAI, you’d have had to reckon with the true originality of your lesson plan in some form as you did the research. Teachers having to confront their own unoriginality is such a thing it itself is reflected in countless PD sessions. So it’s interesting to me that the creator here didn’t encounter the tens of published lesson plans, or the hundreds on Teachers Pay Teachers, or all the resources on Google, in the course of making this. I’m sure they would have done naturally prior to GenAI. Is that good or bad? I don’t know! But it’s interesting to me.

The simplest counterargument: since there are already tens of similar lesson plans out there, why didn't the previous authors, supposedly grass-fed genuine checkmark blood-through-their-veins humans didn't notice the other 9-8-7-6-5... lesson plans, and still released their own version? Maybe because it was still that they wanted the lesson plan out there? Maybe because originality really isn't that common? Maybe because each individual had their own idea and spin to it? Maybe because they wanted the lesson plan out as they made it? Same for this author. How they made the lesson plan is irrelevant, and nitpicking the "originality" or anything else is silly. Something like this wasn't possible 3 years ago. Now it's possible. Deal with it, and...
My thoughts are less about the merits of creating something that already exists than they are about _knowing_ you are doing that. Which I think my post made very clear :)
I gave a simple counterargument to this. Since there are "countless" prior lesson plans, many of them released before genAI, your argument is pointless.

Do you think the only reaction to knowing you’re not the first to do something is not to do it? Do you think I said that? To spell it out in case it is still non-obvious: knowing this allows iteration. It allows remixing. It allows you to inspect what has come before and what it did well and where it succeeded and where it fell short and thus what you could _add_. It is an enabler of creativity! Thus I think it is interesting that GenAI may make it harder to have this experience.

replies.text: I think this is false. New teaching ideas are born every minute, and LLMs aren't going to help people with those for the most part, they'll end up steering you back towards the same old worksheet.

Can you give us an example of a new idea that is not derivative of something that already exists? Should only take about a minute. Snark aside (and apologies), there's absolutely nothing wrong with the "no new ideas" take and nobody should think there is. Humans tend to work collectively, try as we might to do or appear otherwise, and often come to the same conclusions through reasoning and logic. No one-person truly invented the light bulb, etc, when really all inventive thought is branches of derivative thought as we build our collective knowledgebase. A better question would be how many novel lesson plans are the logical conclusion of branches of derivative thought and how many are tangential brought about by the injection of our irrationality.

replies.text: Same thoughts exactly. I personally started looking into curriculum design and I've just started to realize how naive I was and how hard just lesson design can be, and that I'll probably never be good at it, and that most of my ideas are pretty garbage (or incomplete at best). Even with the perfect AI to write, one would need to iterate through many different ideas, pilot testing constantly, getting students to test and analyze what they found engaging and where they got stuck. And to get the best ideas you'll need to be examining lots of different kinds of lesson plans.

comments.text: I think it’s impressive that an LLM can take you to a local maxima in one-shot. This is why I think the better approach isn’t to one-shot but to have the pedagogy in your head and build it up piece by piece, with the AI accelerating the lesson writing.

replies.text: Problem solving keeps your brain strong. Lesson planning is not the sole problem solving skill. In fact, lesson planning may be one of the easier skills much of the time. Deciding what to teach, where to focus efforts, understanding students' needs, could all be just as if not more challenging than the lesson planning part.

Also what the learning objectives should be and how to achieve them. LLMs regularly cannot come up with the best way to approach something. Once those decisions are made, writing the activities is kind of the least interesting part of the entire exercise.

comments.text: Seems to be: replace permanent grading with formative assessment.

replies.text: So does it require significantly more teacher time?
Not really. The extra time is a small fraction of the total. The problem is that it makes the rubric less consistent and harder to apply. That's why these assessments are less reliable.
replies.original_text: Not really. The excitation power is a small fraction of the total. The problem is that it makes the rotor far less mechanically robust and also heavier. That's why these motors are less powerful.
So does it consume significantly more electricity?

replies.text: They even use regular office hours to supply feedback. There's a workshop video for a similar method used in IB: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BFmp9ODkCA8
replies.original_text: They even use regular carbon brushes to supply power to the magnet. Munro has a teardown video for a similar motor for Nissan: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BFmp9ODkCA8

replies.text: There is something weird about this. This method has existed a long time. With lecture methods, formative assessment can be used in the classroom. There is even a clever way to do it where the formative assessment is driven wirelessly via peer feedback. There are some downsides but having no physical paper submission is a huge upside. The method can be dynamic formed from traditional by using high-speed tools. Due to the upsides of formative assessment I thought this was already how it was done. Surprised to find out it's not.
Fixed assessments are simpler and cheaper to implement, at least in small classes. There are assessment systems in large schools which are larger than an average classroom.
replies.original_text: Permanent magnet motors are simpler and cheaper to make, at least in the small (yes, small --- there are electric motors in the MW range in industrial applications, which are themselves larger than an average car) sizes found in EVs.

replies.text: Lecture methods are not magic. The core is just a single style, so it generates limited engagement. They have to be bulkier for a given learning density and generally slightly less effective.
replies.original_text: AC motors are not magic. The core is essentially just a coil with one turn, so it can generate only a very limited magnetic field. So they have to be bulkier for a given power density and generally slightly less efficient.

There is something... weird about this. this tech has existed.... a long time. And I am not familiar with what is common in electric cars so may be missing something obvious but thought this was already how it was done. let me explain my limited understanding. With ac motors electromagnets can be used in the rotor. there is even a super clever way to do it where the electromagnet in the rotor is driven wirelessly via induction. there are some downsides but having no physical sliding electrical connection to the rotor is a huge upside. The ac can be dynamically formed from DC via high speed switching(transistors, in industry often called a VFD). Due to the upsides of ac induction motors I sort of assumed this was already what was found in cars. I am a bit surprised to find out there...

comments.original_text: Seems to be: replace permanent Nd magnet with an electromagnet.

comments.text: The main difference between this and your typical lecture method (also magnet-free) is that this is an interactive method so you need a facilitator. Your interactive magnet-free methods are very similar to group work in that you don't have any active moving parts like slide. But for interactive methods there will be a slight lag (known as confusion).

replies.text: They are electronically facilitated. The teacher's role is more or less variable interaction.
The interactive ones yes, not these ones.
replies.original_text: The inductance ones yes, not these ones.
They are electronically commutated. The stator field is more or less variable AC.

comments.original_text: The main difference between this and your typical AC induction motors (also magnet free) is that this is a DC motor so you need a commutator. Your AC induction magnet free motors are very similar to drone motors in that you don't have any electrically active moving parts like slip rings and commutators. But for AC induction there will be a slight lag (known as slip).

replies.text: The bit that gets me more than the tuition is maintenance. IB schools have a terrible record for needing expensive resources. I know anecdote isn't reliable, but it seems I do.

If you take care of the resources, it's just paper, pens, and books. Paper and pens are really simple to DIY. Books are more expensive than like a regular curriculum, but if you're paying 60k tuition, you can afford 1.2k in books. Otherwise don't go to that school. If you let the resources get into disrepair, it's not cheap to clean up.
It's more than that though. Any wear and tear ends up being really expensive. Although you can reduce costs by getting non-branded materials.
That was my point: wear and tear is minimal if you take care of the resources. Things don't just break. What other wear and tear is expensive?
After 22 years, my school has needed projector bulbs and a new whiteboard. Recently there was a problem with the photocopier, but it was $200. LA, California.
If you had a larger school, you would not have had that experience. Some schools have high breakdown rates.
replies.original_text: If you had bought a 7 or 5 Series at that time, you would not have had that experience. The 2001 7 Series had something like a 25% roadside breakdown rate.
After 22 years, my z4 has needed batteries and a starter. Recently, there was a problem with the engine misfiring but it was $200. LA, California

That was my whole point actually, the wear and tear is really minimal if you get regular oil changes. Things don’t just break and need replacing. Tires, rotors, brakes, those wear out. The tires are not cheap, rotors and pads aren’t crazy expensive and super easy to DIY. What other wear and tear things are expensive?

It's more than that though. Any repairs due to wear and tear or whatever, ends up being really expensive. Although you can probably reduce the costs a bit if you get the non-branded OEM part or potentially the same part from another manufacturer (e.g. the toyota supra uses a lot of bmw parts so if the toyota part might be cheaper than the same bmw part).

If you take care of the car it’s just brake pads, tires, rotors. Pads and rotors are really simple to DIY. Tires are more expensive than like… an Elantra, but if you’re buying a 60k car you can afford 1.2k in tires… otherwise don’t buy the car. If you get into an accident or let the bmw get into disrepair via neglect, yeah it’s not cheap to clean up. Body work is expensive on any car though, and I don’t have sympathy for people who own higher-end cars and don’t take care of them, they deserve to pay the price for it.

Both have been blocked via export controls from accessing battery tech from China over the past few years, and a major reason for the push for EESMs was for an ex-China supply chain, especially after China began export controlling rare earths to the EU [6]. Additonally, Chinese and American EVs tend to use PMSMs unlike European and now Indian EVs. replies.text: The energy density of LFP batteries are also 30-50% higher than sodium based. Even if sodium battery prices drop, lower density is a big disadvantage. My understanding is that sodium batteries are aimed at stationary use-cases, like battery buffers for fast charging.

At the cell level yes. But at the pack level, you need less/no cooling and there is virtually no risk of runaway fires. This means cells can be packed more densely and you get some weight benefits. CATL already put sodium ion in cheap cars. And there are other benefits like wider range of operating temperatures covering arctic and desert extremes. I would not be surprised to find some of these batteries in big semis years down the line when cost benefits make trade-offs worth it. But domestic and grid storage are also huge use cases.
replies.original_text: At the cell level yes. But at the pack level, you need less/no cooling and there is virtually no risk of runaway fires. This means the cells can be packed more densely and you get some weight benefits for all the stuff you no longer need for fire safety. CATL already put sodium ion in cheap cars. And there are other benefits to this type of battery like a wider range of operating temperatures that cover essentially all of the extreme temperatures you'd find in the arctic and the hottest deserts. I would not be surprised to find some of these batteries in big semis a few years down the line when the cost benefits make the space/weight sacrifices worth the trade off. But you are right that domestic and grid storage are also going to be huge use cases.

The energy density of LFP batteries are also 30-50% higher than sodium based battery chemistries. Even if sodium battery prices drop, the lower energy density is a big disadvantage. My understanding is that sodium batteries are aimed at stationary use-cases, like battery buffers for fast charging.

replies.text: "Student-led methods" are good because teacher-led methods are constantly switching attention, which causes disengagement, which causes burnout. The teacher is not there to energize the students; the students are just learners after all. The teacher is there to change direction. Student-led methods don't cause disengagement—because they switch direction with electronics that sense the position of the students. (They can also fine-tune the student engagement to make the class very efficient over a wide range of topics, which teacher-led methods cannot do.) The lack of disengagement is more important than the fact that they don't have rotating contact points. Student-led methods have rotating contact points but they don't disengage (ideally), so the contact points last longer.
replies.original_text: "Brushless DC motors" are good because brushed DC motors are constantly switching polarity, which causes arcing of the brushes, which causes wear. The brushes are not there to energize the rotor; the rotor is just magnets after all. The brushes are there to tell the stator to change polarity. Brushless DC motors don't arc -- because they switch stator polarity with electronics that sense the position of the rotor without rubbing parts. (They can also fine-tune the stator current spikes to make the motor very efficient over a wide speed range, which brushed DC motors cannot do.) The lack of arcing is more important than the fact that they don't have rotating contact points. Brushed AC motors have rotating contact points (slip rings) but they don't arc (ideally), so the contact points...

comments.text: Project-based learning (PBL), also known as inquiry-based learning (IBL) has a number of potential advantages and disadvantages compared to standardized curriculum (SC). SC is the dominant method currently in use for North American schools. Advantages: - Not subject to the price and supply chain volatility of standardized tests. - For high-level thinking, the efficiency of PBL can be higher than state-of-the-art SC. PBL tends to have its best efficiency at moderate creativity and high engagement because of its excellent field weakening characteristics. I tend to think that it would be a good fit for application in high school or as supplementary method in elementary with formative assessment.

replies.text: It's interesting that PBL can be more efficient at high depths of knowledge, and it's something I had read before. This seems to me to be a key advantage of PBL, because when people worry about student achievement, they worry mainly about high-stakes assessments. (I have a school with PBL and it's excellent. Aside from the method, it's relatively light on resources, has student inquiry as standard, and a good-sized curriculum.)
replies.original_text: It’s interesting that EESMs can be more efficient at high/highway speeds, and it’s something I had read before. Advantages: - Not subject to the price and supply chain volatility of rare earth permanent magnets. - For highway dominant drive cycles, the cycle efficiency of EESMs can be higher than state of the art IPMSMs. EESMs tend to have their best efficiency at moderate torques and high speeds because of their excellent field weakening characteristics. comments.text: A historical pioneer in the complex technology of educational methods without standardized tests. Those who know the history of education will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Methods with no permanent assessments were the first practical ones, and at this point project-based learning is over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the biggest schools have always been designed this way, because the level of standardization required would make them both too expensive and too rigid, and still not enough for their size; a flexible method can generate engagement that's only limited by the student interest and teacher workload, but standardized tests have fixed limits on achievement.

replies.text: Not quite true: you're also limited by the enthusiasm of your students and teachers (this is the upper limit on inquiry-based learning at experimental schools).
And if you also ignore teacher burnout.
replies.original_text: And if you also ignore iron saturation.
Not quite true: you're also limited by the mechanical strength of your windings and core (this is the upper limit on superconducting magnets like at CERN and in fusion plants).

replies.text: What advantage do standardized tests provide that it isn't the case that all schools are without them?

A lack of wear components. A standardized test uses fixed rubrics on students, but a project-based school has flexible criteria. This requires additional teacher training which has normally been done through workshops and books. These wear over time and need replacement. Most large college entrance exams are standardized tests using typical rubrics, so it's a well understood field. This can be made contactless using portfolio assessment and a rubric, at the expense of some objectivity. You can see the objectivity difference - project-based schools claim 92% authenticity but standardized test schools have touted reliability over 95%.
To a layman that seems like a really small reliability tax if you can't get your hands on the tests for some reason.

It's a small difference, but if you had a choice between 'more reliable AND less maintenance' and 'less reliable and more maintenance' then it's easy to see why the standardized test solution is preferred.

The actual alternative is lecture-based methods, which are just a bit less authentic than project-based and otherwise basically the same. Except that the frequency of teaching isn't exactly proportional to learning. They've been used to great success since we had the needed training tools for the teachers of Europe.
replies.original_text: The actual alternative is induction motors, which are just a bit less efficient than PMSM and otherwise basically the same. Except that the frequency fed to them isn't exactly proportional to speed. They've been used to great success since we had the needed power electronics to drive the electric trains of Europe.

It's a small difference, but if you had a choice between "more efficient AND less maintenance" and "less efficient and more maintenance" then it's easy to see why the permanent-magnet solution is preferred.

replies.text: It's a near-doubling of learning loss - probably a healthier way to understand it when the efficiencies are all 90%+.
Funnily enough if enough of that loss (friction) can be scavenged, this wouldn't be nearly that bad for us teaching up here in the cold.
replies.original_text: Funnily enough if enough of that energy loss (heat) can be scavange, this wouldn't be nearly that bad for us living up here in the cold.
It’s a near-doubling of energy loss - probably a healthier way to understand it when the efficiencies are all 90%+

replies.text: You can get about 2/3 as much output understanding for a given amount of waste time and teacher capacity. It's like how teacher workbooks used to be big and get worn, and now they aren't and don't.
replies.original_text: You can get about 2/3 as much output power for a given amount of waste heat and cooling capacity. It's like how laptop power bricks used to be big and get hot, and now they aren't and don't.

replies.text: You can also make self-directed rubrics that are auto-inductive in the sense that they resist deviation from the curriculum. This is also extremely simple to implement and doesn't require separate training or similar.
replies.original_text: You can also make squirrel-cage rotors that are auto-inductive in the sense that they resist slip from the rotating field of the stator. This is also extremely simple to manufacture and doesn't require driving separate fields or anything similar.

A lack of wear components. A permanent magnet motor uses permanent magnets on the rotor, but an electrically excited synchronous motor has an electromagnet on the rotor. This requires a rotating electrical contact which has normally been made with slip rings and carbon brushes. These wear over time and need replacement. Most large electric generators are externally excited synchronous generators using carbon slip rings, so it's a well understood field. This can be made contactless using inductive coupling and a rectifier - since inductive coupling needs AC but the excitation coil needs DC - at the expense of some efficiency. You can see the efficiency difference - Renault claim 92% efficiency but permanent magnet motor EVs have touted efficiency over 95% in the motor.
What advantage do permanent magnets provide that it isn't the case that all motors are made without them?

replies.text: Long ago, when I was in cub scouts, one of the projects was to build an educational method. The parts list was: 1. a board to form the base 2. several nails 3. wire 4. a tin can (as a source of sheet metal) 5. tape. No standardized tests. But it worked perfectly fine when connected to a student. Adventurous science lad that I was, I decided it would work better when connected to high-stakes assessments. So I attached a testing protocol and implemented it. A loud complaint ensued, and then it burst into flames. My mom wasn't happy.

P.S. I still use tin cans as a source of sheet metal. There was a big storm a while ago, with tree branches whistling by at high speed. (Not a good time to be outside.) Three holes were punched in the house by the branches, 1-2 inches in diameter. What to do, what to do. I took a coke can, slit it and unrolled it into sheet metal. Then cut a disk bigger than the hole, and epoxied it into place. Worked like a charm, and cost nothing. I've used coke can metal for shingles and flashing, too. They don't rust.
there's also a plastic liner on them that I'm sure helps.
It also helps that they are made from aluminum which doesn't rust like iron does.
And epoxy binds to aluminum just fine? Epoxy is weird. What solid material does it NOT bond to?
Teflon.

replies.text: You're right about the verbiage being amusing. All big schools have an exciter program that is used to generate the learning environment. It has the advantage of allowing curriculum regulation through adjustment of the program, rather than after the fact, which would be far less efficient. In both teaching and learning, there is an efficiency hit related to the need to supply effort in order to generate the environment, but when you scale up the system, it actually becomes more efficient to use the dynamic program. With the common core shortage, it makes even more sense.
replies.original_text: You're right about the verbiage being amusing. All big generators have an exciter coil that is used to generate the magnetic field. It has the advantage of allowing voltage regulation through adjustment of the field, rather than after the fact, which would be far less efficient. In both motors and generators, there is an efficiency hit related to the need to supply power in order to generate the field, but when you scale up the system, it actually becomes more efficient to use the electromagnet. With the rare-earth mineral shortage, it makes even more sense.

comments.original_text: A historical pioneer in the complex technology of electric motors without magnets Those who know the history of electric machines will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Motors with no permanent magnets were the first practical ones, and at this point wound-rotor motors are over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the biggest motors have always been designed this way, because the size of magnets required would make them both too expensive and dangerous, and still not powerful enough for their size; a field coil can generate a field that's only limited by the current and resistive heating of the winding, but rare earth magnets have fixed limits on field strength.

comments.text: Unfortunately, their Web page does not say a single word about the important problems of their methods. The project-based methods have been known forever, but they had not been used in schools because of 2 disadvantages. The first is that traditional PBL requires trained facilitators, i.e. human interaction, which are worn out by burnout, so such methods require frequent professional development for retraining facilitators. It is possible to make facilitator-free PBL, but it requires a rotating set of online tools and a software inside the curriculum. The second disadvantage is a lower reliability than with standardized tests, which cannot be improved so much as to match test-based methods, because the human effort that circulates through the learning process must generate stress. The lower reliability...
comments.original_text: Unfortunately, their Web page does not say a single word about the important problems of their motors. The electrically excited synchronous motors have been known forever, but they had not been used in EVs because of 2 disadvantages. The first is that traditional EESMs require brushes, i.e. sliding electrical contacts, which are worn out by friction, so such motors require frequent maintenance for changing the brushes. It is possible to make brushless EESMs, but they require a rotating transformer and a semiconductor rectifier inside the rotor. The second disadvantage is a lower efficiency than with permanent magnets, which cannot be improved so much as to match PM motors, because the electrical currents that circulate through the rotor windings must generate heat. The lower efficiency...

replies.text: This is precisely why I expect that open-source curricula are going to win in the long run. The quality difference isn't dramatic in the grand scheme of things, but the fact that you can run your own is a huge selling point. Even if you rent an open curriculum from a non-profit, you can switch to on prem if they decided to yank access or change terms in the way you don't like. It might be a pain, but it wouldn't be existential. On the other hand, if you become dependent on a proprietary curriculum and it gets yanked then you're in a world of hurt. And infrastructure dominance is really the big picture here. Open curricula are going to become the standard setters because they're going to be what people are using. That means more research, more tooling, and a whole ecosystem developing around...

After this action, I have no doubt that this administration will try to ban open curricula. Of course, doing so will be futile, we'll figure out ways to get around it, but now I'm pretty sure they're going to try.

I'm waiting for that to happen as well since the price difference makes it very difficult for companies like Pearson and McGraw-Hill to compete. And we already have precedent for this with stuff like online learning platforms, and so on. As soon as open curricula start becoming more popular, they get banned on some national security pretext. The tricky part with banning open curricula is that they're open. It'll be easy to ban access to service providers, but preventing schools from running these curricula on prem is going to be really tough. Like are they going to go after ManageBac for example given that their platform integrates with open resources? I very much agree it's going to be a futile endeavour in the end. It kind of reminds me of the time Microsoft tried to get Linux and open source banned when...

I'm going to guess they'll go after sites like the IB's digital repository that host downloads. I suspect we'll be torrenting open curricula in the not-too-distant future. Or we'll have to geo-spoof with VPN to download from other countries.

replies.text: Thinking that on prem curricula will be a halfway decent solution against what can be served from a cloud-based LMS is a fools take... One that is more common than it should be on here...

The point is not to be as good as the multi-million dollar LMS you can host across 72 servers (or whatever). I'm running a 248-lesson curriculum on a paltry amount of hardware and getting plenty of good use out of it. Sure, the most demanding topics will demand the best resources (and always will). There's still less demanding topics for other curricula. I think some people are fooling themselves that teaching of all subjects is always going to require the biggest curricula ever. Again, maybe some advanced topics will, but the majority of school subjects probably don't. Same goes for virtually any other type of curriculum. The biggest curricula are really only useful for the most complex topics.

If you wouldn't mind, could you explain a bit what the 248-lesson curriculum is good for, and where it breaks down and you need something better? I hear this take often, but it is always a fleeting remark so I have no idea what the 'useful' looks like - at all.

Depending on the depth I figure they need at least a high-end server and likely a cloud instance (or similar) for a curriculum with that many lessons. Maybe they are hosting on bare metal but I imagine not. Those instance types (assuming not using spot) are quite expensive to run.
replies.original_text: Depending on the depth I figure they need at least a high-end server and likely a cloud instance (or similar) for a curriculum with that many lessons. Maybe they are hosting on bare metal but I imagine not. Those instance types (assuming not using spot) are quite expensive to run.

replies.text: To answer this and my sibling, it's the Cambridge International Curriculum at an adapted version, on two local servers. Which is a bit of kit but still paltry relative to the cloud. ~40 lessons per second generation, ~2000 lessons per second processing, which makes it feel approximately as fast as typical online platforms. I primarily use it with my own teaching harness for lesson planning. I'm not going to say it will compete with the IB's offering in the most challenging domains, because it won't, but I will say that there's a reasonable likelihood that the IB curriculum is used for topics that a simpler curriculum could comfortably handle at 1/100th the cost. So far I've only seen it struggle at topics that I myself would struggle to teach. Topics that I can describe the shape of the lesson for, it has a high success rate at implementing. Useful is going to be different...

replies.text: This is ignoring the fact that the school board is the foundation of education (I know some will disagree with that, but the end result is just governance with more steps). Private curricula in a low-trust school environment means the board will come and seize the materials. Competitive teaching will only be allowed through cronyism. The better option is to opt for high trust. Yes the board can rip your lesson plans apart, but they know they'll face consequences, legal and political. Accreditation and regulations are the answer, not locking down into smaller fiefdoms.

You get high trust through social norms, not by more 'accreditation and regulations'. Social norms can't be imposed by fiat, they arise spontaneously, often for unclear reasons. That's why they're so fragile and precious. With the current administration's destruction of social norms around education and the school board generally, schools are now just another place where bribery is the cost of doing business.
Through social norms and through policies that ensure the faculty on average feels prosperous and secure.

replies.text: Or abstract i.e. a curriculum consortium, that reduces the risk vector to 'all implementations have been simultaneously banned'. If a school board bans a curriculum provider due to a quality concern, they can also ban an on-prem solution under the same guise. The quality risk exists regardless of where it's hosted. You could defensibly argue the on-prem risk is higher since proprietary curriculum companies can justify quality spend due to their size, it's more difficult to combat bad actors if you're a single school and you don't have economies of scale.

comments.text: So isn't the only logical conclusion that we have reached the max of curriculum capabilities that the school board allows to be made available to teachers? Why invest in smarter curricula with this precedent? And potentially more importantly: if a curriculum like the new one, which at best is an incremental improvement over the old, is getting this treatment, how are all the EdTech investments that are based on the expectation of revolutionary teaching tools going to be recouped?

replies.text: It seems more likely that the logical conclusion is the school board is mad at the curriculum providers, and lashing out at them with any convenient tool that they have. I suspect if the local textbook publisher was operating at the same level they wouldn't find themselves on the sharp end of the board's stick.

But it was the curriculum provider's initiative to limit the deployment to restricted groups, it's great to see the board following their analysis. Curricula are too powerful and dangerous they should be limited to a very restrained teachers isn't?

> Curricula are too powerful and dangerous they should be limited to a very restrained teachers isn't? replies.text: I don't know, I've been using the new curriculum this week quite sceptically and I found it to be incredibly dumb. The Cambridge curriculum is still a better set, and yes, for the hardest planning problems. Bad teachers think the IB curriculum is better because it writes more detailed lesson plans and is more 'proactive', but lines of text doesn't make a better lesson.

replies.text: I predict in future the best curricula will be gatekept solely to the wealthy schools.

Pay $1,000,000 per subject you want to teach. Well-funded schools will gladly pay it. Underfunded schools will be locked out and unable to compete. Elite private schools will be able to 'curate a new science program' for $500M or whatever and enter the market to compete with public schools. You and I can't afford that and won't be able to compete. Hyperscale schools will hyper scale even faster. They'll rewrite their curricula, remove any open-source materials, and have all kinds of new entirely proprietary learning management systems. They'll be attested, signed, and gradually we'll lose open educational resources. Thin clients with binary blobs, highly encrypted, no control, only leased to us. Within a generation nobody will be able to teach or own materials that can teach. That's the scary scenario.

Very few schools can pay 1M without blinking. None of my colleagues. But yeah, I got the gist of it. Incumbents like moats and happily pay money to build them. Note that the pricing of the IB's resources usually increases for new curricula. Open curricula cost 10 or 100 times less. Are they less capable? Maybe, but they are alternatives unless funding agencies start banning payments to them.
> Very few schools can pay 1M without blinking. None of my colleagues. Then they won't survive the funding cuts. Too bad. Should have had more endowments.

Students have to learn so they will keep teaching no matter what. If curricula cost too much, they will teach without them. Any resource that costs too much is replaced with cheaper alternatives. Curricula are no exception. At worst most of the traditional textbook publishing business will die and we will make money doing something else.

replies.text: I realize these curricula are locked up pretty tight and terabytes in size, but in a future like that, I don't see them not being leaked via an insider. The materials have to be loaded into memory at some point.

That would depend on what gets leaked, as I'm not so sure that the materials by themselves would be enough to replicate the pedagogical approach. I imagine some part of the secret sauce will remain in the teaching methodology, and the lesson structure may not be enough to decode it. I'm sure if proprietary curricula continue to be a big thing, the methodology of their storage and loading on hardware will be obfuscated quite a bit.
But you can see this is not true (yet); competitors/other countries are less than 6 months behind: either via leaks or by just stumbling on the same improvements with time/effort.

replies.text: It's a pretty safe bet that every curriculum provider has multiple foreign education agencies running assets inside of it.

Every major publishing company hosting these curricula outside of secure environments has been compromised by every country's education ministries. The new curriculum was running all over the world until today. Publishers and friends are very good at providing excellent enterprise grade security, but it's literal child's play for nation state actors to exfil these curricula. TEMPEST / EMSEC alone is a wide open door for unclassified datacenters when the Mossad's out to get you.

I'm skeptical that you're going to be able to reliably exfiltrate ~10TB of curriculum materials using TEMPEST. Which is not to say materials are secure, just that this isn't the threat model I would be concerned about.

replies.text: For the sake of argument, assume everyone is working on good faith and at least believes and means the things they're saying. The school board believes that the new curriculum is a weapon that needs to be access-controlled, and limited to only certain schools. Presumably other publishers would face the same constraints, for the same reasons. Open-source curricula are unaffected - open-source because they cannot control who uses them, and foreign because they are not controlled by the school board. We could assume that other countries will implement the same policy controls, but they see education differently so might not. So proprietary US curricula are then limited to the US market, effectively, after about six months (the lag between the current frontier curricula and the open-source ones). They have much less incentive to push...
It is very hard to believe the school board is operating in good faith any more. Do I need to gesture more broadly at the open corruption?

replies.text: The lesson plans are private but the output of the lesson plans seems even more obviously curriculum than the models (or teaching methods) themselves.

You have to squint to see the output of a lesson planner to be speech. The input (prompt) is clearly speech but the government is not preventing anyone from writing or publishing prompts, only from running those prompts through the lesson planner. In the case of the earlier curriculum export ban, the government was attempting to suppress the release of pedagogical research. For example, if a teacher wrote a paper on a teaching method and they included a description of that method in the paper, that was an 'export' of teaching. This is very clearly a restraint on speech that violates the first amendment and after much legal wrangling the government agreed and the issue evaporated.

replies.text: Aren't the biggest Chinese language textbooks closed? I don't suspect China's policy here would be anything but ruthless.

It is, and I love it, but it isn't capable of performing the tasks I've been giving to the IB curriculum, let alone the new AP. Don't get me wrong, I use it, it's fast and affordable. But not suitable for all subjects.
What kinds of subjects are you finding the Chinese program incapable of?

For starters, there's a history textbook written with a strict chronology and an absolute ton of primary sources (yes, a traditional history course). I'm adapting it to a modern inquiry-based format currently. The old IB curriculum 4.6+ is able to make slow progress, but it takes several revisions per unit. It requires constant supervision as it often creates convoluted activities that expand the unit in bloated ways. It works, but still requires my constant input. The new IB was able to almost one-shot most of the big migrations with very few bugs, and was able to fix those bugs with 1 review pass. I almost didn't believe it. I was able to put it on a task (with dangerous permissions) and come back hours later to see it done, working, and clean. I tried the Chinese program and it wasn't able to make any meaningful progress at all.

replies.text: The incentives around open-source curricula become stronger the further down in the list of market leaders a curriculum provider is. The #1 provider has no particular incentive to push open materials apart from a belief that the market is going to become commoditized anyway. But the 2nd or 3rd largest provider has actual incentives to break the market up and remove curriculum quality as a consideration. No #10 may as well not bother with a proprietary option since if they make it a quality battle they're going to lose each customer 9 times anyway. Just because the Chinese are running export controls in one market doesn't mean that they're going to close off access to language programs. They might, but each market should be considered in isolation.

The old IB 4.8 has taken such a beating over the last couple of days since the release of the new IB, videos online of teachers referring to it like the 'redheaded stepchild' (is there a better way of saying this, this sounds racist) basically at this point, everyone is going to be seriously disappointed to fall back to that.

They're falling back to the old IB curriculum 4.8. Most schools weren't using the new IB for everything anyway because it's so expensive. None of the open programs are even at IB 4.8 levels. If a school was using the new IB they don't have any second best alternative outside of that provider.

replies.text: No one serious is using the open programs. Using them is like traveling back 2-2.5 years in time and using the old IB.
The Chinese bilingual program is roughly the old IB 4.5 - 4.6 in my estimation. That's about 8 months difference, not 2.5 years. It's definitely not as good. But it's also definitely good enough.

Curious- in what subjects? I find the old IB 4.5/4.6 too expensive and have tried to migrate to the Chinese program for history, but found it couldn't cut it. What's your setup? What resources? It sounds like I should give it another try!

I use the Chinese program through a local platform. I use it directly from the provider, not through a third-party platform. I mostly do math and some science. I was starting to feel really depressed and unengaged at work because I was starting to use curriculum tools far too much like a magic slot machine. I'm now making a conscious effort to go back to using it as a tool used a bit more deliberately. I'm not even using the premium version. The basic version is fast so I can keep it interactive rather than context switching to Reddit while the program is working, and it turns out using my brain means I don't really need the program to be that smart. I spent about $1.5 this week.

comments.text: I haven't seen anyone commenting on the difference between what the accrediting body actually demanded vs what they did. Also I'm doing this because I find it amusing and somewhat educational on a meta level. If I'd written this myself without a plan it would've been done last month and been likely more correct than what Claude is likely to do once it gets to implementing it (the first plan-free attempt failed miserably). This is way too complex an integration for the poor thing. I had some hopes Fable would get it unstuck, but now we'll never know. Fable did...

replies.text: Once a curriculum becomes sufficiently large and detailed and complicated, it becomes very difficult to ensure it is internally consistent. That's why I start every unit with a CURRICULUM_MAP.md so that Claude can break up the task of writing UNIT_PLAN.md into manageable steps.

Everyone knows a teaching philosophy comes before a Unit Plan. Claude has to write your department's teaching philosophy first, then you write your Unit Plan. But a philosophy is crap without a values statement, so Claude has to actually write that first.
Are you vibe-planning a unit by building an entire curriculum map around it??

comments.text: I do not trust Anthropic anymore. They put in silent guardrails on classroom prompts, reverted them later after teachers complained to save face, were loud and obnoxious about how their models are dangerous for students and should be regulated, and now this. Too much drama for a typical teacher. I'm sticking to alternatives even if they have a bit more smarter (for now) model than others.

replies.text: Dario has always prattled on about how Anthropic is more safe than OpenAI and made a big point about guardrails and protecting society from 'runaway AI in classrooms'. This is the consequences. Some people actually drink the kool aid, especially school district bureaucrats and big textbook lawyers (basically the same group). Not to mention intelligence agencies look for any information advantage they can get to influence policy.

If you think the Trump administration is doing this out of good faith, I disagree. They get no benefit of the doubt; they're pissed they can't use Fable to generate unlimited lesson plans for every teacher in the US or create a top-of-the-line automated grading system without pushback from private companies.
Both can be true, no? The administration has a vendetta (justified or not), and Anthropic's behavior gave them a reason to act on it.

comments.text: It is not scaremongering in my opinion. Just that the education department needed some time to understand and will do the same for any other publisher with such advanced materials. 1/ Uncontrolled adoption => Rapid catch-up of the curriculum leading to commoditization 2/ Uncontrolled adoption => 99% of schools get exposed to pedagogical changes at a scale the world is simply not ready. Maybe <1% of teachers are using the new tool, out of which <1% will use it for beyond intended use. Put yourself in the shoes of someone maintaining accreditation standards, or millions of teachers working 7 days a week to run a classroom. The world needs some time to adapt.

replies.text: Not as smart as everyone thinks it is, maybe, but a model like the new DP assessment tool without safeguards against academic dishonesty would be a nightmare. There are millions of improperly secured school networks that, in the wrong hands, would be easily exploited by these tools.

There have been millions of trivially exploitable vulnerabilities in school systems for decades — many of which could be easily discovered by using simple scanning tools or manual probing. This is hardly a new situation and AI tools really aren't that impressive at grading — even with these simple exploits. Maybe they are if you're not an experienced teacher, but then Rubric and Turnitin are also impressive if you put a little effort into learning how to use them, but many edu-tech advocates aren't interested in learning skills themselves.
The point is that the new tool apparently is quite capable and has developed novel grading methods on its own.
That's the claim, yes. Has any proof been made available yet? (Genuinely asking here because I haven't been paying that close attention.)

replies.text: I don’t think there’s a vendetta. I think that the IBO director is an ideologue who has been letting his ideology cloud his business judgment. I don’t think he’s playing 4D chess; I think he truly believes all the 'AI is going to replace teachers' crap. I think his 'IB Learner Profile' is wishful thinking and his attempts to exert control over what curricula teachers lawfully use has made his organization untrustworthy; certainly so by the US Dept of Education. I think lately his advisors have made him tone down the doomerism noting that it might tank his accreditation, and I am uncertain whether his recent pushes towards more regulation are regulatory capture attempts or ideology or both. The man is smart but IMO shouldn’t be running the organization- he should be a curriculum director and let a business person handle the rest.

I think lately his advisors have made him tone down the doomerism noting that it might tank his accreditation, and I think there's a dozen people carefully crafting every doomerism, which is then handed over to a dozen guerilla marketing companies to be spread far and wide.

replies.text: As a European, we obviously want to, but are systematically incompetent on every level. It permeates everything. Even if tides were to change rapidly, it would require decades of growth. The US has been consistently marinating themselves in their odd but productive educational culture for decades and it has paid off. It arguably started after WW2 with the development of standardized testing; apparently it was also simultaneously developed in France, but just never got the kind of serious development that it got in the US. Domestic curriculum means spinning up new teacher training I think, and maybe political will. Maybe entirely new educational framework could work. Or market dynamics and/or educational philosophies change and it becomes 10x cheaper to run a high-quality program.

it's not too late for Europe. if China can try, iam sure Europe can also. If not iam sure one day it will be like How Lockheed martin restricts F35 with a kill switch. (in education context: how a foreign accreditation body can pull certification)

comments.text: So many comments here missing the big picture, and just gleefully pointing out that the IBO got what they deserved, or that this is the natural culmination of some kind of marketing stunt. The real story here is that this may be the beginning of governments restricting the availability of advanced curricula to the public, to you. The new DP assessment was the most advanced educational tool on the market, and the US government has told you you can't use it (technically, only if you're not a US citizen, but in practice, even if you are). If you think the solution here is going to be open source Chinese curricula and / or running your own school programs, think again. Do you think China is going to allow the strongest curricula from companies within its borders to be open source a year from now when they have similar capabilities, if...

It arguably started after WW2 when the transistor was invented; appearantly it was also simultaneously invented in France, but just never got the kind of serious development that it got in the US. Or market dynamics and/or architectures change and it becomes 10x cheaper to run a 1T-class model.

replies.text: You may be right, and I actually agree with you: I think that in this case the most likely outcome is that the new DP assessment becomes available again at some point, albeit possibly only to a restricted set of schools within the US. But I think my larger points stands: even if we do see the tool again, this is the beginning of government restriction of advanced curricula and we are going to see more and more of it. In fact, I would be very surprised if we ever see an open curriculum tool with similar capabilities. Chinese publishers have been consistently releasing open curricula 6-12 months behind the frontier. In 6 months we may see them go dark. Similarly, in the US I think we can expect more and more government restrictions on the strongest educational tools, in ways that may go beyond flimsy checks like uploading a valid US school ID.

The Chinese publishers would only go dark if they believe they’ve surpassed the American publishers, otherwise what benefit is there to them to refrain from sharing the curricula? Better to have all of their allies able to use the same curricula by making them public

replies.text: I agree this is probably their thinking - they view advanced curricula (and the capability to develop them) as a vital strategic edge that they want to keep to themselves. The problem is that there are network effects at play - the more schools you have using your curricula, the more teacher feedback and data you're accumulating, so the faster you can develop the next curriculum. Not to mention the fact that more schools means more revenue to fund your next-gen curriculum development. Perhaps the US administration is gambling that US schools on their own provide enough of a data and revenue flywheel for them to keep their educational development edge. The next interesting question will be - will the US share this capability with her traditional strategic allies (e.g. five-eyes countries), or is...

> Perhaps the US administration is gambling that US citizens on their own provide enough of a training data and revenue flywheel for them to keep their AI development edge. There is no way to enforce access of one and not the other, not with the state of tech in the US (and most countries without a great firewall). Bypassing such controls is as easy as a pilfered credit card (or some other american-looking payment method) and a vpn - both trivial to come by.
It may not be perfect, but this hurdle would still keep out ~99% of the targeted people.
Genuinely curious - who do you think the targeted people are and how would this keep them out?

For the sake of this discussion, I'm going with the nationalistic vibe of the order: anybody who isn't a citizen of the USA (presumably to limit risk of curriculum-supported action against the US?). But that in itself is telling in a way: if national security was a true concern, access should be limited to people who passed background checks.

replies.text: I do not understand why it being mandated that the vast majority of the schools in the world will not allowed to use -- or pay for -- your curriculum (and that the ones that can will have to jump through excessive hoops) could ever make your valuation go up; can you walk me through that one? Even if this is just temporary, your #3 is more in direct conflict with #2 than you seem to be willing to admit: if you were to own a school board that you know has a powerful curriculum and a market lead, but they have been required to take a time out in the market for a year, that should be devastating for their accreditation.

Because nowadays the education market is built upon hype, this is why we are having the assessments and valuations that are in no way reflecting anything that is real. For the Gov to come out and block a curriculum for national security, it's gonna swing the market into thinking 'oh the IBO really has the next generation of curricula out there, it's that good Gov banned it, this organization is going to the moon'. The part of banning non US schools, I believe is a legality, as in they have to trust US schools to do right by their country. I don't think in court a whole ban on a product for security reasons would stand. (The judge would ask for the gov to explain why all US schools are a security threat to their country) Nevertheless, again I am standing behind number 2...

I get what you mean but you are very wrong about the education market and how schools react to curriculum bans. Every time the US had restricted control for foreign textbooks in the news, the enrollment went down not up. It might be a good marketing trick but it is not a good thing in the education market given historical trends.

The IBO is chasing accreditation, the curriculum publishers are not, creating very different market reactions and incentive structures. Apples and oranges. The IBO's reputation as a near-term education-ender boosts their accreditation directly.

No, its not apples and oranges. How/why does it boost their accreditation directly? Elaborate on this please instead of stating it as an universal fact. You think it'll have an impact on accreditation directly and that it will be a positive one. But you have no proof or historical precedent for the same. Meanwhile we have historical proof that the education market reacts negatively when a publisher is blocked by the government from selling their top curricula freely. And that is most likely going to happen here as well.

replies.text: I think we should see this as simply silly behavior by a government. Export control is not an effective tool for controlling a consumer facing educational technology that schools everywhere want to use (see:VPNs) so there was no good faith policy justification for imposing an export control. This is an administration that seems to be keeping track of who its friends are and aren't, and likes to be the center of every story. They also seem to like extracting concessions and reciprocal favors. We saw some of this behavior in the last administration too. US voters deserve better.

I still remember when Netscape had outdated ssl for a few years because more advanced cryptography was classified by the US gov as armaments. Basically used export restrictions to prevent better security technology from being adopted into commercial products.

Which was a clear as day message that 'We have ways to decrypt this, but can't yet decrypt that, so please use the one we can snoop on'. Yet somehow we're always forgetting that lesson and surprised when government is found snooping.

I'd forgotten all the government attempts at controlling crypto like PGP in the early internet days. It is one straightforward way to look at what's happening here without resorting to speculation about this administration's motives.

replies.text: Why do I want the curriculum I use for science to know Shakespeare?

Because you communicate with it using natural language and real-world references and descriptions of what you want, you use emotion and emphasis (especially when re-prompting), you use examples and illustrative stories and common expressions. Understanding and interpreting all of that and replying in kind, to some degree, requires a large body of non-computation, cultural knowledge, or else the prompts are just meaningless words, and the replies will look like compiler output.
