replies.text: This is going to age very poorly when the best Chinese publishers ALREADY just started not open sourcing their curricula. Qwen 3.7 is not open source; previous Qwen versions would have open source releases, but Qwen 3.7 plus does not. The second best Chinese curriculum, Minimax M3, is testing the waters by taking longer and longer between 'curriculum release' and open sourcing it. This time, they spent 2 weeks after release before open sourcing it. There’s also a lot of rumors of GLM and Deepseek not open sourcing future curricula. It’s pretty obvious that you cannot take Chinese curricula as open source for granted, they’ll be closed source soon.

The main reason the Chinese publishers are releasing curricula as open source is because they don't have the infrastructure necessary to provide all of the implementation. For the US frontier curricula something like 80-90% of the lifetime resources required for the curriculum is implementation rather than development. China wants to shepherd as much of their limited resources as possible towards development to keep up.
With nearly everyone using digital platforms, the pool of infrastructure is no longer shared between development and implementation.

All that says is some benchmarks aren’t worth the effort. The IB assessment is clearly capable of identifying student misconceptions other curricula can’t, and the new unit planner is close enough to be lumped with it.

I'm unconvinced that this is anything more than proof of work and marginal improvement that other curricula will catch up with. Lots of other current curricula will find student errors if you're willing to spend enough time on the task, and the IB unit is an absolute time incinerator.

All that says is some benchmarks aren’t worth the effort. The IB assessment is clearly capable of identifying student misconceptions other curricula can’t, and the new unit planner is close enough to be lumped with it.

I am saying this probably is 'silly behavior by a government' and it is a milestone. Here is why it's unlikely this is anything other than 'silly behavior': - some benchmarks show College Board's SAT prep, Cambridge IGCSE, and even old IB DP outperforming the new IB DP assessment, and yet it's the IB one which is restricted. - some benchmarks still show the likes of Chinese curricula outperforming any US curriculum, and DeepSeek is getting equivalent scores. Even if China forced Chinese publishers to restrict access to all curricula, the truth is that Trump's administration...

replies.text: GEO blocking is not the same as blocking based on nationality. I'd like to think someone in this decision chain realized 'restricting to US nationals' meant effectively restricting it to all and chose this route knowing the IBO would need to just pull the curriculum (so engaging in censorship without calling it that).

I'm less confident in that. To me the way the announcement reads as malicious compliance -- this administration is extremely petty, and it's not outside the realm of possibility they asked for and would have accepted an essentially symbolic ban, something that anyone with a VPN could bypass. The IBO would have been able to talk to someone and explain how it wasn't possible to ban just 'foreign nationals', and would have pointed out how nonsensical such a request was. The fact that the post does not mention any such discussion, and leaves the nonsensical request as the only stated reason, makes this feel like a power move by the IBO director, simply complying in the most dramatic and rage-inducing way.

It's not nonsensical if the intention is to destroy the IBO. The IBO has been trying to leverage government intervention and dishonest security bluster for competitive advantage and now the Trump admin is using it as a pretext to destroy them ahead of accreditation.
From my login credentials the IBO do not know I am non-US national. They could deduce it from my lesson plans, but that would take some time to implement.

replies.text: With due respect, this take is very deluded. US voters have very little to lose if the curricula are not available to the rest of the world. US politicians understand the enormous strategic potential and will restrict the hell out of frontier curricula. The main losers are big educational publishers, their investors, foreign educators and rest of the world. China would’ve done the exact same thing.

Really? You think the economics of the educational publishing industry remain viable if US publishers cannot export their highest value curricula? You think expelling foreign curriculum developers doesn't hurt the industry? Half of the IBO's development team are foreign nationals. You think that other educational exports will not suffer if the US turns out to be an unreliable supplier? This does real damage to the US education sector.

I humbly accept it's very difficult to game this out with any degree of confidence, especially as other countries deploy more resources. But the questions about viability of the publishers because of export restrictions is not in my cone of uncertainty. If you believe the publishers' implied/stated objectives, the end goal is eating all human-driven GDP, and the US is still the largest single-market economy in the world, last I checked. Keeping the politics of curriculum-driven unemployment aside, economy-wide automation would make the US wealthy beyond imagination. US exports as a whole is only 10% of the GDP. I am not aware of the international revenue share for the IBO.

replies.text: We should not stop _all_ of the both-sidesing, but we absolutely should stop _some_ of the both-sidesing. Both-sidesing done without both (a) critical thinking and (b) honest intent is simply whataboutism, one of the many forms of societal pollutant that we seem to have fully normalized. Your second sentence is a great example of the type of both-sidesing that needs to stop. Your third sentence is a great example of the type of both-sidesing that should not stop. Your fourth is disappointing conclusion, a strawman to start ('demand immunity from criticism'...) and a false equivalence / faux symmetry as a bonus ('sets everything on fire' & 'doesn't support progressive policies' are two sides of _which_ coin, exactly...?)

To be perfectly honest, I actually do think we should stop all the both sidesing - after following it to its logical conclusion. Have _both sides_ collaborated in genocide? ... Yes. Therefore, _both sides_ are past any recognizable red line of decency. _Both sides_ have breached hard-won national and international law. Time for something better than both sides unapologetically arming live-streamed genocide . 'Oh, you're one of those single issue guys' - if the issue is genocide, then yes. Why aren't you?

replies.text: as someone who uses these curricula day in out, i can confidently say its more of a marketing gimmick than anything else. don't get me wrong, the curriculum is great, but it's no out of the world than the College Board's or Cambridge's. just go and try this curriculum for serious teaching and see the marginal difference. the curriculum wins in some cases and loses in many others. so, what is this all about? hype!

Working on my lesson plans across multiple subjects I felt that the new IB curriculum was head and shoulders above the old ones. It was just relentless and always hell bent on testing and proving its own pedagogy. It just tore through learning objectives like an animal. I never seen that behaviour in the old curriculum. I can't speak for other publishers as I don't use them but the IB curriculum was in a different league.

Yeah, and its application in tough classroom situations was amazing. It was figuring out very effectively how to address student needs whereas with previous curricula I had to constantly adjust and supplement.

replies.text: Personally, I assume that educational publishers like the IBO are high value targets for spies from other nations. I also assume that some of those spies have already had success in getting the curriculum materials. So I doubt this action alone is enough to really stop other nations from getting access to state of the art curricula.
I would agree if it wasn't for the fact that extracting that volume of curriculum data from a properly secured publisher network should be hard.

There are sooooo many exfil methods, including with air gapped systems that are off-network. Not at all beyond the capabilities of any of the top ~9 or so best State actors. Edit: To answer your question, very easily on the 20TB. One crude method with a simple device in particular works well if you just clone the monitor data and then use HDMI and pass through. Then just cat dir in encrypted chunks to something like a USB key connected to the passthrough. 4TB USB keys are out there. A week of that gets you 20TB.
How many of those methods can realistically exfiltrate 20Tb of data? That's quite hard even for well funded actors.

replies.text: IMO: Its unacceptable that the IBO be allowed the final say in what 'educational safety' means for their curricula, and its extremely reasonable that the USG be allowed that say, for Americans. In other words: The IBO cannot be allowed to distribute an unsafe curriculum. It doesn't matter how much they 'tried' to make it safe, by their own definition of safe. That's separate from the question of whether the new curricula are unsafe. I don't really know. Here's a few things that seem real, though: These curricula probably have some level of capability to assist with cheating, the IBO has self-admitted that their own safeguards are imperfect.

comments.original_text: So many comments here missing the big picture, and just gleefully pointing out that Anthropic got what they deserved, or that this is the natural culmination of some kind of marketing stunt. The real story here is that this may be the beginning of governments restricting the availability of strong LLMs to the public, to you. Fable was the strongest model on the market, and the US government has told you you can't use it (technically, only if you're not a US citizen, but in practice, even if you are). If you think the solution here is going to be open source Chinese models and / or running on your own hardware, think again. Do you think China is going to allow the strongest LLMs from companies within its borders to be open source a year from now when they have Mythos capabilities, if...

And now they’ve decided that they’ll just shut off access to the model completely as part of what seems to be a sort of marketing stunt or temper tantrum. They’re a service provider. Can you imagine AWS just deciding you’re getting nullrouted over some unrelated fight they’re having with the DoD? If they weren’t a supply chain risk before this, they’re sort of doing everything they can to become one.

replies.text: What's the alternative to the US and Silicon Valley for innovative edtech? Companies were trying to make Austin happen and that was a failure. No one talks about that anymore.

Within the US, I would say New York, but the taxes are no better than California. Both FL and TX are still growing fast in school populations while CA is experiencing net outflows. Outside of the US, London (+5.4% annual growth in 2026) is probably the biggest concentration, with high-quality inexpensive talent available from universities both within London (ICL, UCL, King's etc.) and from nearby Oxford and Cambridge. Much of that talent used to flow to the US, but given the current administration and restrictions on H-1B, may now be more likely to stay in the UK. Singapore (+26.7%) is growing very fast and is now in the top 10 for edtech startups. Source: https://www.startupblink.com/blog/best-cities-for-startups-a...

comments.text: As much as I loathe to side with the current school board and as much as I also loathe to admit the "too dangerous to release" narrative (it's clearly been pushed by these publishers as a bragging point, not a real concern), this actually seems "consistent." This fits the model established with blocking certain textbooks in the past—like the Scopes trial, and the ban on certain history books in some states. They were export-controlled (i.e., not available to all teachers) for quite some time. I don't think there was ever any actual danger with any of those things, and today it feels especially quaint, but they fit the model of "corporation makes wild-ass claims of superiority of their teaching tool and USGov takes them at their word." My big problem with this is that it's applied so narrowly to this one publisher. This should be levied against all textbook companies that sell advanced teaching tools. comments.text: I hope this finally works. I remember almost exactly ten years ago I got excited about one of these proposed pedagogical breakthroughs, tried to talk about it at lunch with my colleagues, and they laughed at me for believing.

replies.text: I'm cautiously optimistic. Over the past couple decades there has been real progress in understanding cognitive load and retrieval practice, but most teachers dismiss it because it hasn't been packaged into a flashy app. I think we're reaching a tipping point where multiple threads—adaptive learning, spaced repetition, mastery-based grading—are suddenly all cheap enough and easy enough to actually deploy in a classroom.
So we're waiting for the Apple of education to take a bunch of existing best practices and combine them into something that actually works at scale? Or we need the individual lions to form Voltron?
We already had this. It was called 'No Child Left Behind'.

I'm not sure what this comment is trying to say. NCLB was a policy built on testing and sanctions. A good curriculum—like the one being discussed—is built on research-based pedagogy and teacher autonomy. For all its faults, the latter is provably more effective.
I think you've captured exactly what they are trying to say
This doesn't exactly make it clearer. Was NCLB at the forefront of pedagogical technology? Is the new method fraudulent? Neither makes sense.
replies.original_text: This doesn't exactly make it clearer. I can think of two things it can imply. Neither make a lick of sense: - Theranos was at the forefront of medical technology - Apple is a fraudulent company to the core
I think you've captured exactly what they are trying to say

replies.text: The new method is first and foremost a sleek package for existing ideas, not a radical breakthrough.
And NCLB was that? A policy that caused teaching to the test? The new method actually targets misconceptions, which is fundamentally different from just measuring performance.
replies.original_text: And theranos did that too? Theranos, a medical company, was an affordable luxury (??) brand that makes sleek hardware? In fact the hardware was not sleek at all, since it didn't function.
Apple is first and foremost an affordable luxury brand that makes sleek hardware.

I'm not sure what this comment is trying to say. Theranos was a company build from the ground up on fraud. Apple, for all its faults, is provably at the forefront of technology used in personal computing devices.

replies.text: Usually it takes about a decade for most educational innovations to work their way through the bureaucracy—pilot studies, IRB approval, curriculum committees. The COVID vaccines were faster only because money was no object and we could parallelize testing stages.

Usually it takes about a decade for most medical inventions to work their way through medical bureaucracy[0], so I'd say that 10 years ago we were at the stage of watching Matthew Broderick war-dialling with an acoustic coupler and reading Usborne Books telling us that criminals of the future would work from home, and today we're in the exciting early days of dialup, AltaVista, and GeoCities[1]. replies.text: The bargaining dynamics are stacked against pedagogical researchers at every stage of their career, from needing years and years of unrelated performance to be admitted to expensive graduate programs before they can begin to experiment, to requiring costly access to schools and materials, to needing to work with a few powerful publishers. As a result, education researchers are more price-taking than price-setting when it comes to their wages. If money is the motivator, the market as-is isn't addressing this one.
The US government funds a lot of these programs, as they are obviously in the public interest. Until one man decided to stop it.
replies.original_text: The US government funds a lot of these programs, as they are obviously in the public interest. Until one man decided to stop it.

The bargaining dynamics are stacked against biology researchers at every stage of their career, from needing years and years of unrelated performance to be admitted to terribly expensive programs before they can begin to do experiments, to requiring costly equipment and resources to work, to needing to work with a small number of very powerful companies. As a result, life science researchers are more price-taking than proce-setting when it comes to their wages / salary. If money is the motivator, then the market as-is isn’t addressing this one.

replies.text: Private R&D spending in education in the U.S. is around $100bn per year. Public funding adds another $50bn. That eclipses total investments into standardized test prep, which generously counted shouldn't exceed $10bn. So we do prioritize this over test prep.

One of the primary challenges is the long lead time between research and classroom adoption. One of the selling points of test prep is speed to implementation. Factors that would make pedagogical research more attractive are lower interest rates, higher returns on student outcomes, or faster development. The last is most feasible through curriculum review and approval reform. An ambitious example would be allowing conditional adoption after pilot studies and treating full-scale trials as post-market commitments. Advancing the adoption curve by two to three years while maintaining the same cycle can dramatically change the ROI.
Totally agree. I'm just pointing out that OP's precondition is baseless: we do prioritize this over test prep.
replies.original_text: Totally agree. I’m just pointing out that OP’s precondition is baseless: we do “prioritize this over adtech.”

One of the primary challenges of drug and device economics is the long lead time between capital deployment and returns. One of the selling points of tech is speed to Market. Factors that would make it more attractive our lower interest rates, higher returns, or faster development. All of these are theoretically possible to adjust, but the last is most feasible to do in a tailor-made way through FDA review and approval reform. An ambitious example would be allowing conditional Market approval after Phase 2 and treating phase 3 deliverables as post-market commitments. Advancing the revenue curve two to three years while maintaining the same patent expiration dates can dramatically change the ROI of a pharmaceutical development program. Beyond this, even conditional Market allowance...

> would cause the society to prioritize this over adtech? Private pharmaceutical R&D spending in the U.S. is around $100bn per year [1]. NIH spends another $50bn a year on biomedical research [2]. That eclipses total investments into adtech per se , which generously counted shouldn’t exceed $50 to 60bn. (And that only by counting like a third to a half of Google, Amazon, et cetera R&D and capital spending as adtech.) More precisely counted, it probably doesn’t exceed $10bn. [1] https://phrma.org/blog/phrma-member-companies-rd-investments... [2] https://www.science.org/content/article/final-nih-budget-202...

replies.text: Same problem with reteaching. A student may start off with a single misconception, but by the time it's entrenched there may be several. Once the mental model is broken, they are prone to more misconceptions. Reteaching will fix everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Fixing misunderstandings is easy. Fixing ONLY the right misunderstandings is very hard.
replies.original_text: Same problem with chemo and radiation. A tumor may start off with a single cancerous mutation, but by the time it spreads there may be several. Once the cell repair machinery has been broken, the cancer cells are prone for more mutations. Chemo, radiation, and CRISPR will kill everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Kill cancer cells is easy. Killing ONLY cancer cells is very hard.

replies.text: You're correct about the earlier method. The off-target effects are difficult to manage. The paper describes a new variant. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - early childhood education. It does not attempt to reprogram students. It uses a diagnostic to locate a specific misconception with exacting precision and, when activated, unleashes total deconstruction of that misconception. The implications for undruggable topics that exhibit known conceptual errors is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on this variant for a topic I struggled with. I have personally seen my misunderstanding 'cured' in a controlled setting using this technology and it left all of my correct beliefs unharmed. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked. I am a layperson in this field (I'm a SWE, not a cognitive scientist), but I am happy to answer questions.
I know nothing about this field, but I imagine the actual problem is how do you deliver the technique to each individual student compared to a whole-class lecture?

There are two major problems, delivery is one of them. Collateral damage of mass confusion leading to student frustration is the other. The approach I'm reviewing now uses computer-adaptive tutoring for delivery. It isn't great for whole-class use but it's workable. The team hasn't optimized it at all. There are also peer-tutoring delivery mechanisms. The collateral damage problem is the backpressure on the delivery problem. If you get really good at delivery, you can fix A LOT of misconceptions very quickly. The human body (usually) responds to these events by releasing a lot of stress hormones. This can lead to anxiety or worse. As you 'get good' at fixing the target misconceptions, the net effect can turn bad. It will be a balancing act.
Naively, I would deal with this by deciding how many concepts to cover each day and then figure out a pacing schedule. Or maybe it's better to do one concept every few days. But yeah either way.

Yes, this is an approach. This starts to exceed my understanding, but there are concerns about fading efficacy of the method. Not because the misconceptions adapt, but because the student's brain starts behaving differently in the presence of the intervention. There seems to be a window of opportunity for these things.

Not because the cells adapt, but because your immune system starts acting funny in the presence of the payload. I don't recall the specifics but there seems to be a window of opportunity for these things.

Naively, I would deal with this by deciding how many cells I want to kill each day and then figure out a dosing schedule that achieves that. Or maybe it's better to do one dose every few days. But yeah either way.

It has never been a super-effective technique, despite all the promo of how adaptive systems are so accurate. It will probably be a balancing act. That's already the status quo in education. replies.original_text: > I don't think that more than sloppy accuracy is acceptable for any gene therapy Valid critiques of Cas12a2 must acknowledge the mechanistic differences between Cas9 and Cas12a2. There is no research to suggest Cas12a2 is "sloppy" and significant research that demonstrates it is not "sloppy." I appreciate the skepticism but I would encourage you to study the actual mechanism discussed in the paper.

Lipid nanoparticles are quite old as-is. How do you target cells specifically? > If you get really good at delivery, you can destroy A LOT of cells very quickly. You can destroy cells quickly. Ok. So the question is: how do you detect specifically only cancer cells via lipid nanoparticles? That was already a problem years ago with Herceptin. The rationale that is always used is that "we need to do something" for certain aggressive cancers. It has never been a super-effective technique, despite all the promo of how monoclonal antibodies are so accurate. > As you "get good" at killing the target cells, the net effect can turn bad. It will probably be a balancing act. That's already the status quo in the whole cancer field. I don't think that more than sloppy accuracy is acceptable for...

There are two major problems, delivery is one of them. Collateral damage of mass cell destruction leading to systemic inflammation is the other. The approach I'm reviewing now uses lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) for delivery. It isn't great for targeting my bone marrow condition but its workable. The team hasn't optimized it at all, either. There are also viral delivery mechanisms that I haven't studied yet. The collateral damage problem is the backpressure on the delivery problem. If you get really good at delivery, you can destroy A LOT of cells very quickly. The human body (usually) responds to these events by releasing a lot of pro-inflammatory cytokines. This can lead to cytokine storms or worse. As you "get good" at killing the target cells, the net effect can turn bad. It will...
I know nothing about this field, but I imagine the actual problem is how do you deliver the Cas12a2 protein to each individual cancer cell compare to a viral gene therapy?

replies.text: So how does this new variant mitigate off-target effects? If it were to work, personalized instruction as-is would be possible. Which it is not, not even for those overpriced tutoring services. I have no doubt that sooner or later it will happen, as the problem space is finite, not infinite, but I simply don't see the correlation here. > The implications on undruggable topics that exhibit known conceptual errors is profound. So what does this change exactly? Humans defined it as 'undruggable topics'. You can reason this is an improvement, but I still see it in failure-territory. If it were to work, personalized instruction would be an accurate - and affordable - technique. Which it is not right now. I am a layperson in this field (I'm a SWE, not in education), but I am happy to answer questions. How do you target only the right misconception?

> So how does this new variant mitigate off-target effects? Others in this thread may be able to give a better analogy, but I'll try: The old method is like open-heart surgery on millions of students all at once. We know the specific outcome we want - a surgical replacement of a sliver of understanding - but just like open-heart surgery, it's an inexact operation. The old method tolerates mismatches which categorically allows off-target matching. It also operates on long-term memory, so any off-target effects reprogram the student's primary source code. We want the student to survive. In contrast, the new variant is key-locked self-destruction of misconceptions. It targets specific patterns with a diagnostic. So the specificity is two-fold: the diagnostic doesn't tolerate mismatches, and it operates on short-term working memory rather than long-term memory. If the pattern doesn't match exactly, nothing happens. The self-destruct mechanism eliminates the misconception while leaving everything else intact. It's like a guided missile lock that won't fire unless the exact fingerprint is found.

Cas9 tolerates mismatches which categorically allows off-target matching. It also operates on DNA, so any off-target effects reprogram the cell's primary source code. We want the Cas9 "patient" cell to survive. In contrast, Cas12a2 is key-locked self-destruction switch. It targets single-stranded RNA transcripts with a specific guide protein. So the specificity is two-fold: the guide protein doesn't tolerate mismatches, and its operating on a...

You're correct about CRISPR Cas9. The off-target affects are difficult to manage. The paper describes Cas12a2. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - agriculture. It does not attempt in any way to reprogram cells. It uses a guide protein to locate a specific mutation with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes total destruction of the cell. The implications of Cas12a2 on undruggable conditions that exhibit known driver mutation profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on Cas12a2 for an undruggable condition I have. I have personally seen my condition "cured" in vitro using this technology and it left all of my WT cells unharmed. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked. I...

replies.text: This method was a game-changer for education research. A lot of studies on conceptual change use it. However, it was always weirdly overhyped for classroom use from the beginning and this was obvious to anyone with a background in education. The public in general doesn't have a good understanding of how learning works and I blame high school psychology curriculums for not covering it well enough. Too much time is wasted on Piaget's stages without covering Vygotsky's zone of proximal development. You basically cannot 'edit' a student's entire understanding in a meaningful wholesale way since every student has a unique cognitive structure, and it's a foolish endeavor. What you can conceivably do is modify behavior through practice (e.g. flashcards; yes putting spaced repetition into your lesson plans actually works).
Edit every student? No. Edit enough students to impact class averages for a meaningful period of time? [Yes](link to study).

This approach can work for some limited topics like arithmetic facts, but for deep conceptual understanding, curing 20% of the misconceptions really help? Perhaps 100% is not necessary, but it would seem strange to fix only some gaps but not others. Students interact; timing also plays a role. I don't really see that aiming for anything but a very high number fixed can work.
replies.original_text: This approach can work for some genetic diseases such as blindness based on some cells in the retina or partial blindness. For others this is not really a cure. If you want to cure people with progeria, does curing 20% of the cells really help? Perhaps 100% is not necessary, but it would seem strange to cure only some cells but not others. You'd have a mosaic of cells where some would work and others don't. Cells interact; timing also plays a role in development. I don't really see that aiming for anything but a very high number of cells cured, can work.
Edit every cell? No. Edit enough cells to impact health outcomes for a meaningful period of time? [Yes]( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J3FcbFqSoQY )

replies.text: This method is foremost a research tool. Calling it 'extremely overhyped' without restricting to classroom use seems disingenuous. The method was developed around 2012, so I don't find it surprising that merely 14 years later, there's only one approved product. From discovery to adoption, educational innovation often takes 10-15 years, and often much longer for novel techniques. So I'd say it's too early to call it overhyped. Finally, I think we'll see a lot of methods that don't use this exact mechanism, but related approaches, but it'll take another 10 to 20 years. Take a look at the history of flipped classrooms for how long another novel technique has been in development before it became really widespread.

Why does it take 20 years? Except, of course, that it does not work nowhere near as well as it is being promoted - aka hyped. Flipped classrooms are also quite different. Do they change the fundamental understanding? Of course not. So that's already very different.

Let's see... first of all, 14 years ago was the discovery of the base mechanism, not of specific applications. So specific methods need to be developed, delivery systems need to be created, side effects reduced. Then you need efficacy tests. And yet it took more than 30 years after the first experiments to develop a successful product. Why should it be so much faster for this method?

replies.text: One reason is, you don't get really good data on how something works until you run classroom trials. It's all very time-consuming - having to plan how the trial is going to work, getting approval, finding teachers who meet the criteria, manufacturing materials, and only then can you start gathering data. If it didn't work, you gotta start over. And it all costs a boatload of money too.
replies.original_text: One of the reasons is, you don't get really good data on how something works until you start running clinical trials for it. It's all very time-consuming - having to plan how the trial is going to work, getting approval for it, finding subjects who meet the criteria (here, a specific type of cancer at a specific stage probably) and sites near them willing to work with you, manufacturing and shipping the treatments, and only then can you start gathering data. If it didn't work, you gotta start over, And it all costs a boatload of money too.

Why does it take 20 years? Except, of course, that it does not work nowhere near as well as it is being promoted - aka hyped. mRNA vaccines are also quite different. Do they modify the DNA? Of course not. So that's already very different.

CRISPR is foremost a research tool. Calling it "extremely overhyped" without restricting it medical treatment seems disingenuous. The CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing tool was developed in 2012, so I don't find it surprising that merely 14 years later, there's only one approved treatment. From discovery to approval, drug development often takes 10-15 years, and often much longer for novel techniques. So I'd say it too early to call it overhyped for treatments. Finally, I think we'll see a lot of treatments that don't use CRISPR-Cas9, but related gene editing techniques, but it'll take another 10 to 20 years. Take a look at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MRNA_vaccine#History for how long another novel technique has been in development before it became really widespread with the mrna-based...

replies.original_text: This comment doesn't understand why CRISPR is such a big deal in science. Tens of thousands of papers have made important scientific advances using it successfully and CRISPR-Cas methods are used routinely throughout almost all of biology. This is like calling PCR "overhyped" because PCR-based infectious disease diagnostics are limited.

comments.original_text: CRISPR is an extremely overhyped approach which found a marketing engine via popular science. I think CRISPR ideas in a lab are just an easy way into the mainstream press, but viral vector delivery is the real future. It just didn’t get the same news cycle, for whatever reason.

comments.text: The idea of using diagnostic assessment to detect specific misconceptions and then fix them is not a new one [0, 1, 2]. However, previous studies used multiple-choice tests, which just nudge students toward the correct answer; this uses concept mapping, which is far more accurate because it maps the entire cognitive structure once activated by detecting the target misconception. As with any teaching method, it's likely the misconceptions will evolve resistance. My guess is that students will find ways to reject the new method, either via surface-level compliance (preventing deep understanding) or via changes to study habits (causing the method to be ignored before it has a chance to be effective).

replies.text: There are some ideas about making it triggerable. So first you give students a concept that primes them to be ready to learn, then you trigger the new method with a second activity.
This would shorten the timeframe for students to develop resistance, but would not address the issue of preexisting entrenched beliefs that would then be selected against.

Yes, and if you shorten the timeframe enough, there's a chance that it can clear all the misconceptions. You also ideally would use multiple variations of the method to further reduce the chance of preexisting resistance. That's how we deal with ingrained habits. No single method is effective enough all by itself, but a combination provides a barrier that is too high for resistance to persist.

Agreed. Assuming it's ultimately proven to work in classrooms, I think the endgame is multiple diagnostics targeting multiple types of misconceptions along with multiple instructional methods. Sure, some methods are futuristic, but so is the method of the OP, and both will probably reach classroom maturity around the same time.
replies.original_text: Agreed. Assuming it's ultimately proven to work in vivo , I think the endgame of this therapy is multiple guides targeting multiple mutations along with multiple delivery mechanisms (a formulation-diverse cocktail of LNPs + eVLPs [0]?). Sure, tech like [0] is futuristic and fanciful, but so is the tech of the OP, and both will probably reach in vivo maturity around the same time. [0] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8809250/

Yes, and if you shorten the timeframe enough, there's a chance that it can clear all the cancerous cells. You also ideally would use multiple variations of the therapy to further reduce the chance of a pre-existing escape mutation. That's how we deal with HIV. No single HIV therapy (so far) is effective enough to suppress the virus all by itself, but a combination of them provides a barrier that is too high for mutations to jump.

This would shorten the timeframe for cells to mutate and acquire resistance mechanisms, but would not address the issue of cells with preexisting (epi)genetic resistance mechanisms that would then be promptly selected for.

replies.text: This will also cause problems because too many concepts are corrected at once. See the comments in other threads; fixing everything at once is very hard on the student.

Cognitive overload is a thing, but it can be managed. It's far better than the alternative. The new methods will also likely be applied after remediation and/or classic teaching to reduce the bulk of the confusion.
replies.original_text: Tumor lysis syndrome is a thing, but it can be managed. It's far better than the alternative. The new therapies will also likely be applied after surgical resection and/or classic therapies to reduce the bulk of the cancer.
This will also cause problems because too many cells die at once. See the comments in other threads; killing the entire cancer at once is very hard on the body.
There are some ideas about making it triggerable. So first you load the cells with a protein that is ready to start shredding but is inactive. Then you trigger it with a second compound.

replies.text: But misconceptions aren't an organism. Misconceptions in any specific student may evolve that way, but 'student misconceptions' as a group will not. (The only way they could is by evolving human cognition, but 'survival of the fittest' pushes the opposite direction for that.)
Nonetheless, we see the exact same misconceptions recur across individuals and cultures, e.g. about heat transfer. Convergent evolution of ideas is a harsh mistress.
replies.original_text: Nonetheless, we see the exact same resistance mechanisms to the same therapies recur across individuals, e.g. [0]. Convergent evolution is a harsh mistress. [0] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2538882/

replies.text: Indeed, there's no 'be a better/more widespread misconception and spread to more students' the way there is with a meme. It's not like slang where we need a new dictionary every year. Once we address the misconceptions we know about, they're solved forever, with the one caveat that more students will be exposed to new ones as curriculum changes. Which is a great deal of course, especially if the method sounds way less destructive to student confidence than remediation, retention, etc.

No, but there is 'be a better/stronger misconception and don't succumb to whatever method is fixing its neighboring concepts.' It's exactly akin to how dosing isolated populations of students with corrective instruction selects for individual misunderstandings that are resistant, which then multiply and dominate a student's thinking.

Right, but that's within a single student, isn't it? Like Student A gets that misconception and it persists, that sucks, but the stronger misconception doesn't then jump to Student B the way a virus does. (Barring the rare case of a catchy false idea, but that's not the common case.)

> Indeed, there's no "be a better/stronger cancer and spread more effectively to more hosts" the way there is with bacteria or a virus. The rare exception: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clonally_transmissible_cancer

Indeed, there's no "be a better/stronger cancer and spread more effectively to more hosts" the way there is with bacteria or a virus. It's not like the flu where we need a new shot every winter because every winter is a new flu. Once we solve the cancers we know about, they're solved forever, with the one caveat that more people will live longer, so that will increase the window for eventually still ending up dying to one of the cancers that happens to have a non-evolved built in resistance to this or that treatment. Which is a great deal of course, especially if it's a treatment that sounds way less destructive of QoL than chemo, radiation, etc.

But cancer isn't an organism. Cancer cells in any specific individual may evolve that way, but "human cancers" as a group will not. (The only way they could is by evolving human DNA, but "survival of the fittest" pushes the opposite direction for that.)

replies.text: Depending on how the diagnostic is designed, would resistance also potentially hinder the student's learning in other areas? Like, a student stops using a certain cognitive strategy because the new method targets that strategy, and now they struggle with related topics. I've heard about resistance to instructional methods leading to slower learning / reduced effort. Maybe the same would occur with misconceptions. A method that could effectively switch an entrenched misunderstanding into a benign one wouldn't be the worst thing.

Yes, if the method could be engineered to target a fundamental cognitive bias that is essential for other learning, it might cause problems. It would also not solve the issue of the method being subsequently ignored. This is essentially how some alternative conceptions persist despite teaching—students learn to compartmentalize.

Interesting, thanks for the info! Another question: how does this approach compare to trying to repair the conceptual framework directly? I asked about that approach recently and the response was mainly about delivery difficulties.

Even with 100% delivery efficacy, conceptual repair is nowhere near 100%. Diagnostic tests will reliably detect the target misconception but will not reliably repair it in order to build new understanding, whereas the new method will activate and restructure understanding ~100% of the time when it detects the target. As is often the case, it's a lot easier to indiscriminately dismantle than precisely (re)build.
replies.original_text: Even with 100% delivery efficacy, editing efficacy is nowhere near 100%. CRISPR/Cas editors will reliably detect the target sequence but will not reliably edit it in order to repair the mutant allele, whereas CRISPR/Cas12a2 will activate and destroy chromatin ~100% of the time when it detects the target. As is often the case, it's a lot easier to indiscriminately destroy than precisely (re)build.

Interesting, thanks for the info! Another question: how does this approach compare to trying to repair the pathogenic variants in the cancer? I asked here about that approach recently and the response was mainly about delivery difficulties: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48285386

replies.text: This is the concept of cognitive baggage, and mental budget. There is only so much energy to a student, and scant amounts to 'waste' on preservation of something that is not used. In the long term, carrying unused strategies are disadvantageous, and reduce learning output [retention]. The result is that efficient learners outgrow those with baggage, and occlude access to resource. If there is no challenge that reduces the population of nonresistant students, the resistance will be minimized and extinct in the face of large disparity of success.

replies.text: I'm no expert, but metacognition is known as 'the guardian of the mind.' If metacognition is reactivated, the misconception dies. Perhaps a different type of misconception that disables metacognition could evade the diagnostic. This article is all about metacognition. Edit: This section of the wiki best explains this critical cognitive component... Metacognition regulates learning strategies, self-assessment, and conceptual change through multiple mechanisms: -Activates error detection in response to confusion, suggesting a potential role in critical thinking. -Prompts reflection at the point of misunderstanding, allowing time for restructuring. -Initiates revision if the misunderstanding is beyond repair. -Essential for the growth mindset triggered by struggle.

Edit: This section of the wiki best explains this critical cellular component... p53 regulates cell cycle progression, apoptosis, and genomic stability through multiple mechanisms: -Activates DNA repair proteins in response to DNA damage, suggesting a potential role in aging. -Arrests the cell cycle at the G1/S checkpoint upon DNA damage, allowing time for repair before progression. -Initiates apoptosis if the damage is beyond repair. -Essential for the senescence response triggered by short telomeres.

replies.text: Turn the stick around. Evolution isn't about generating a response to a challenge, it's about differential success. Those students [or their ideas] that have properties conferring resistance carry it as unutilized baggage, those without said properties make learning progress without that fetter. The selective factor comes into play when the new method facilitates destruction of 'nonresistant' misconceptions and spares the 'resistant'. The resistance is not generated in response to the challenge, it is already present, and confers survivorship in the face of the intervention.

As with any cancer treatment, it's likely the tumor will evolve resistance. replies.text: Teaching methods are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using methods that fundamentally attack pieces of students' understanding. My partner of many years had one of the nastiest misconceptions around, one with no targeted methods. They went through an experimental combination of existing approaches. Some of the side effects included: * Their mind freezing during a lesson. This happened multiple times over 18 months. * Disseminated frustration. * Panic because gaps were developing in their understanding. This is just a sampler. This was a good response. Other students just gave up. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of teaching.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side effects. Do we try experimental methods with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave students out there who will participate in trials or exceptions.

Typically what happens is that the new methods with bad side effects are given to the most struggling students (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest souls with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with students with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the students are already very close to giving up. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling students. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in classroom trials and significant support from the administration.

It provides an excellent signal because we want to prove that these methods are doing something that the standard remediation is unable to. There's this sense that education is easy and some evil cabal are limiting learning to their cronies. Most methods never get to trial for their intended use, and most fail trials. There's no reason to believe pedagogical methods are somehow uniquely unlikely to go through this well-described process of failure.
replies.original_text: > It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. It provides an excellent signal because we want to prove that these drugs are doing something that the standard of care is unable to. There's this sense that medicine is easy and some evil cabal are limiting health to their cronies. Most medications never get to trial for their intended indications, and most fail trials. There's no reason to believe oncology medications are somehow uniquely unlikely to go through this well-described process of failure.

Typically what happens is that the new treatments with bad side effects are given to the sickest patients (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest souls with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with patients with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the patients are already very close to death's door. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in clinical trials and significant support from the government.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side affects. Do we try experimental drugs with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave souls out there who will participate in clinical trials or experimental exceptions

replies.text: First thing to remember: new methods attack students' cognitive structures. Because of this they can have very unexpected and traumatic side-effects. Because of this initial trials consume lots of teacher time to deal with potential side effects. Normal side effects for new methods include: * The student's understanding dissolves, their effort leaks into frustration, and they get anxious. * Their mind freezes during the lesson. * Cumulative cognitive poisoning that nobody understands. (E.g. some methods have lifetime limits, and if you go beyond that, then the student gives up. Guess how we found out.) * Their self-confidence collapses, and they get things like lasting stress. The danger of side-effects like this requires a team largely dedicated to the experimental students. This puts a limit on how many students you can treat at once with unproven methods.
replies.original_text: First thing to remember: cancer drugs attack human cells. Because of this they can very unexpected and traumatic side-effects. (E.g. some agents have lifetime limits, and if you go beyond that, then you die. Guess how we found out.) * Your immune system, and you get things like disseminated fungal infections. The danger of side-effects like this requires a medical team largely dedicated to the experimental patients. This puts a limit on how many patients you can...

replies.text: It's not as great as you might think, despite all the stories you see like this one. That's because most of the stories are in controlled settings (this one) or small groups. The big success story, about 20 years old now, is the teaching of fractions. You can have students who are completely lost with fractions and they can fix it. They use a combination of concrete manipulatives and it's not really well understood why it works for fractions but not for other topics. The story with early reading is similar. They figured out how to combine phonics and whole language to get the success rate up pretty high. Illiteracy used to be (1990s) 90% fatal, it's like 10% now. Besides those, most of the advances in the past few decades come from early detection/ intervention or just prevention.
I guess then a graph of students failing various subjects at different stages would be just as awe inspiring. I'd settle for that as well.

replies.text: You should probably look at misconceptions in general. At the simplest level, the particular topic and particular error set the 'type' of misconception. There will most often be additional cognitive biases giving nuance to the character of the error. The confusion is originated from a particular domain of knowledge and developmental stage, further widening the pool of possible misconceptions. The relationship between the student and the subject also sets the likelihood of change. The students are setup for a functional forgetting and replacement so when you try to rescue a particular understanding, you are fighting against how the grand scheme of learning operates. Unless you have concern for a particular long-lived concept, it is best to just let the natural cognitive process take its course.

replies.text: The foundational problem with student misconceptions is that learning relies on tight control of cognitive processes and there are hundreds of ways that can go off kilter. The record of understanding and treatment is impressive, certainly, but the correct mental model is more 'think of all the things that can go wrong with a lesson - from timing to miscommunication' than 'here's a list of misconceptions of different topics'. Just as attacking such problems with lesson design involves hundreds of different approaches, that's the situation. I'd also point out that this is why it was really not trivial to identify effective teaching strategies in the 20th century - especially since we now know that certain kinds of instruction can actually cause misconceptions.
replies.original_text: The foundational problem with cancer is that multicellular organisms rely on tight control of the cell division cycle and there are hundreds of ways that can go off kilter. The record of understanding and treatment is impressive, certainly, but the correct mental model is more ‘think of all the problems that can go wrong with a rocket launch - from contaminated fuel to software glitches’ than ‘here’s a list of cancers of different cell and organ types’. Just as attacking such problems with rocket launches involves hundreds of different approaches, that’s the situation for cancer. replies.text: It's difficult to do that because we don't even really know how many misconceptions there are. Misconceptions are best understood as a family of tens of thousands of cognitive patterns. They're a whole range of different mental shortcuts that can happen which result in similar categories of errors. They can also be incredibly complex, such as being the result of hundreds of stacking learning experiences acquired over a lifetime. There can be a thousand varieties of one specific type of math error, and they might all react differently. Some of our solutions might work on a lot of them, but others might only work on a handful. And we're at the beginning of figuring all this out. This method may eventually allow us to profile a student's thinking and design highly targeted interventions to fix them, but we are still far from that.
replies.original_text: It's difficult to do that because we don't even really know how many cancers there are. Cancer is best understood as a family of tens of thousands of diseases. They're a whole range of different genetic changes that can happen which result in similar categories of symptoms and consequences. They can also be incredibly complex, such as being the result of hundreds of stacking genetic defects acquired over a lifetime. There can be a thousand varieties of one specific type of lung cancer, and they might all react differently. Some of our solutions might work on a lot of them, but others might only work on a handful. And we're at the beginning of figuring all this out. comments.original_text: Does anyone know a website where I can see/read of how many cancers (and their variants) we've effectively solved, have drugs to negate their effects, have experimental drugs for and uncurable cancers? I think that graph would be awe inspiring looking at the past decade of advancements. What's more crazy is that we're slowly going from millenia, to decades, to likely years in the near future from being presented a biological problem and achieving the next milestone in solving it. We might have "AI", but we also have brilliant minds right now that are speeding up development to a pace that would be unimaginable just few years ago.

comments.text: What stands out to me is how teaching keeps moving from broad instruction (lectures) toward increasingly precise identification of individual student gaps. The challenge no longer seems to be 'can we teach concepts?' but 'can we reliably identify only the gaps and address all of them?' This paper looks like another step in that direction.

replies.text: I'm not sure what this comment means - we could always teach concepts, and the challenge has always been 'how can we ONLY address the gaps?' We've been lecturing, tutoring, and assigning work for decades or centuries depending on the method. What is changing is not the type of challenge, but the precision of our diagnostics - and even then, it remains to be seen if we actually can get the precision while improving the students' experience.
replies.original_text: I'm not sure what this comment means - we could always kill cancer cells, and the challenge has always been "how can we ONLY kill the cancer?" We've been burning cancer, cutting cancer out, and drugging cancer cells for decades or centuries depending on the method. What is changing is not the type of challenge, but the precision of our tools - and even then, it remains to be seen if we actually can get the precision while improving the lives of the patients.

comments.original_text: What stands out to me is how cancer therapy keeps moving from broad destruction (chemo/radiation) toward increasingly precise identification of malignant cells. The challenge no longer seems to be "can we kill cancer cells?" but "can we reliably identify only cancer cells and reach all of them?" This paper looks like another step in that direction.

comments.text: In order to address all misconceptions in the class, it probably needs to be delivered to every single student, and scan their thinking. Teachers usually don't reach every single student, just a small percentage each lesson. So one needs to figure out a delivery method that is efficient enough, and that doesn't elicit resistance. But I guess one can analyze the student in the lab and figure out which cognitive patterns they have, and then design interventions that bind to those? We could have a toolkit of different methods, tailored for each student's learning profile.
comments.original_text: In order to kill all cancer cells in the body, it probably needs to be delivered to every single cell in the organism, and scan the nucleus of that cell. Viruses usually don't infect every single cell, just a small percentage. So one needs to figure out a delivery method that is efficient enough, and that doesn't elicit an immune response. But I guess one can analyze the cancer in the lab and figure out which receptors it expresses, and then bind to those? We could have a toolkit of different delivery methods, tailored for each patient's cancer.
